skill.name: esm
skill.description: Guides protein design and analysis with ESM3, ESM C, and the Forge API. It covers sequence generation, structure prediction, inverse folding, embeddings, batch workflows, and responsible validation steps.
skill.summary: Use ESM protein language models for sequence design, structure prediction, inverse folding, and embeddings.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported critical heuristic risk, but review found no malicious code execution, C2 behavior, credential exfiltration, or prompt-injection attempt. Most high-volume findings are Markdown examples, protein-model terminology, API documentation, or non-security cache hashing. The main publishable warning is unsafe pickle deserialization in cache examples and normal remote Forge API token usage.

medium_findings.title: Unsafe Pickle Deserialization in Cache Examples
medium_findings.description: The documentation shows cache and checkpoint helpers that load pickle files from local paths. This is legitimate scientific workflow guidance, but pickle loading can execute code if a cache file is attacker controlled.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct examples call pickle.load on cache files. The risk depends on whether users keep those files trusted, so this is a real but contextual issue.

low_findings.title: Remote Forge API Token Workflow
low_findings.description: The skill documents Forge API calls with token parameters and external service URLs. This is expected for the skill, but users must keep tokens in secret storage and understand that protein inputs may be sent to a remote service.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The remote API and token pattern is explicit and legitimate. I found no evidence that the skill hardcodes real secrets or sends data to unrelated endpoints.

low_findings.title: Static Code Execution Findings Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The eval and shell-execution hits are in JSON audit text, Markdown code fences, model methods, installation snippets, or Python string formatting. I found no executable skill script that runs dynamic code or shell commands automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited files are documentation or generated audit metadata, not executable hooks. The ESM method name eval means model evaluation mode, not arbitrary code evaluation.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and C2 Keyword Findings Are Contextual False Positives
low_findings.description: MD5 appears as a cache-key helper for protein data, not as a security control. C2 and reconnaissance terms map to model tracks, batch control, data analysis, or printed workflow labels rather than command-and-control activity.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations show cache hashing and ordinary workflow output. MD5 collision resistance is not relied on for authentication, authorization, or integrity enforcement.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pickle Cache Loading From Local Files
dangerous_patterns.description: Example cache code uses pickle.load for embeddings, checkpoints, and Forge results. Use only trusted cache files or replace pickle with a safer serialization format.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code pattern is present in documentation and is known to be unsafe for untrusted files. No evidence suggests malicious intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: External API Calls With User Tokens
dangerous_patterns.description: Forge examples initialize a remote client with token parameters and send protein prompts for inference. This is normal capability documentation, but it creates privacy and credential-handling obligations.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly use a remote API and token placeholder. There are no real tokens or unrelated exfiltration endpoints in the reviewed evidence.

content.value_statement: Protein design workflows are complex and model-specific. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical ESM guidance for generation, embeddings, structure work, and Forge API use.
actual_capabilities: Explains ESM3 sequence, structure, and function generation workflows.
Guides ESM C embedding creation for clustering and downstream models.
Shows local model setup and Forge API client usage patterns.
Covers inverse folding, structure prediction, and function conditioning.
Provides batch processing, caching, checkpointing, and error-handling examples.
Includes responsible-use reminders for generated protein validation.
limitations: It is documentation guidance and does not include executable tooling.
Large ESM3 models require Forge API access or suitable hosted infrastructure.
Generated proteins require computational and experimental validation.
Some cache examples use pickle and should only read trusted local files.

use_cases.title: Prototype new protein designs
use_cases.description: Generate masked sequences, add function annotations, predict structures, and refine candidates before lab validation.

use_cases.title: Analyze protein libraries
use_cases.description: Create ESM C embeddings, compare variants, cluster sequences, and select representatives for deeper analysis.

use_cases.title: Build protein representation workflows
use_cases.description: Use ESM C outputs as features for property prediction, classification, similarity search, or transfer learning.

prompt_templates.title: Start sequence generation
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the ESM skill to plan a simple ESM3 sequence generation workflow for a 150-residue protein. Include model choice, inputs, and validation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Create embeddings
prompt_templates.prompt: Use ESM C to compare a small protein sequence library. Recommend an embedding workflow, batching approach, and clustering method.

prompt_templates.title: Design from structure
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan an inverse folding workflow for a target PDB structure using ESM3. Include sequence generation settings and checks before ranking designs.

prompt_templates.title: Scale Forge inference
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Forge API batch pipeline for protein generation with retries, checkpointing, token handling, cache safety, and cost controls.

output_examples.input: Plan a workflow to generate GFP-like protein candidates.
output: A staged ESM3 plan with function conditioning, sequence generation, structure prediction, refinement, and validation checkpoints.
Model selection guidance for local prototyping versus Forge inference.
A reminder to screen candidates computationally and validate experimentally before use.

output_examples.input: Compare 500 protein variants with ESM C embeddings.
output: A batching and embedding plan for efficient processing.
A clustering approach for selecting representative variants.
Safety notes for storing caches and avoiding untrusted pickle files.

output_examples.input: Move an ESM3 workflow from local testing to Forge.
output: A migration checklist covering model selection, token storage, rate limits, retries, and remote data considerations.
Guidance on when to use smaller models, medium Forge models, or large research models.

best_practices: Start with smaller local models before scaling expensive Forge runs.
Store Forge tokens in environment variables or a secrets manager.
Validate generated proteins with independent computational checks and experiments.
anti_patterns: Do not load pickle cache files from untrusted locations.
Do not treat generated sequences as experimentally ready designs.
Do not send confidential protein data to remote services without approval.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps plan ESM3 and ESM C workflows for protein generation, structure work, embeddings, and Forge API usage.

faq.question: Does the skill run models directly?
faq.answer: No. It provides documentation and workflow guidance for using the ESM Python SDK and Forge API.

faq.question: Can it work with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The marketplace metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: When should I use Forge API?
faq.answer: Use Forge for larger ESM3 models, scalable batch inference, or production workflows that exceed local GPU capacity.

faq.question: What should I know about security?
faq.answer: Protect API tokens, avoid untrusted pickle cache files, and review any generated workflow before execution.

faq.question: Are generated proteins safe to use?
faq.answer: No generated protein should be used without biosafety review, computational screening, and experimental validation.
